{{Shrine | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
Honden (October 2018){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = Former village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | Alternate names = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] {{Shrine | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
Honden (October 2018){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = Former village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
Honden (October 2018){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = Former village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=draft}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|12=draft}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|12=draft}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|12=draft}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=draft}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏|12=draft}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=draft}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = Former village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=draft|12=draft}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|12=draft|12=draft}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|12=draft|12=draft}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|12=draft|12=draft}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=draft|12=draft}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏|12=draft|12=draft}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=draft|12=draft}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

revision.comment: 45 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市) up to rev 100502381
revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=2015年10月27日 (火) 01:04 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 三熊野神社 |画像 = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 (2018年10月){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} |所在地 = [[岩手県]][[花巻市]]東和町北成島5区1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[伊弉冉命]]
[[事解男命]]
[[速玉男命]] |社格 = 旧[[村社]] |創建 = 伝[[延暦]]21年（[[802年]]） |本殿 = 切妻造鉄板葺 |別名 = 熊野神社 |札所等 = |例祭 = 9月19日 |神事 = 十二番角力式（泣き相撲）（9月19日）
全国泣き相撲大会（4月下旬） |地図={{Location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=三熊野神社|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}}}} '''三熊野神社'''（みくまのじんじゃ）は、[[岩手県]][[花巻市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]である。正式名称は'''熊野神社'''（くまのじんじゃ）。 == 祭神 == [[紀伊国]]の[[熊野三山]]から[[勧請]]されたと伝える[[伊弉冉命]]、[[事解男命]]、[[速玉男命]]の3柱を祀る。 == 歴史 == 社伝によれば[[坂上田村麻呂]]が対[[蝦夷]]戦争（[[蝦夷征討]]）に際して、征矢立（せいやたて）の森に登って紀伊の熊野三山に戦勝を祈願し『岩手県の地名』。、戦勝後の[[延暦]]21年（[[802年]]）にこの地に三山の神を勧請して創祀したという。また、[[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]）に[[源義家]]が[[前九年の役]]で[[安倍貞任]]を追撃した際には[[鏑矢]]を奉納して戦勝を祈願し、[[奥羽]]平定を叶えたともいう。 中世には[[和賀氏]]から[[社領]]70[[石 (単位)|石]]を寄進され、[[江戸時代]]になると[[元和 (日本)|元和]]4年（[[1618年]]）に[[南部藩]]藩主[[南部利直]]より社領23石が安堵された。 近世には「熊野権現宮」と呼ばれ、境内に隣接する毘沙門堂（成島毘沙門堂）の[[鎮守]]とされ、古くからともに'''熊野山成島寺'''（じょうとうじ）を[[別当]]としていたが、[[明治]]初年（19世紀後葉）の[[神仏分離]]で成島寺を廃寺とした際に独立し、毘沙門堂とも分離した。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。 == 祭祀 == [[File:Mikmano-jinjya dohyo,Hanamaki.jpg|thumb|泣き相撲土俵 (2018年10月)]] 9月19日の例祭に奉納される'''十二番角力式'''（じゅうにばんすもうしき）は[[幼児]]による'''泣き相撲'''として知られる角力（[[相撲]]）神事で、鎮座地の南を西流する[[猿ヶ石川]]を境に南北に分かれる北南両成島集落のそれぞれから長男で[[数え年]]2歳の幼児6名宛を[[力士]]として選び、本殿脇の[[土俵]]上で対面させる以下、本節は別注記を除いて門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」（高橋秀雄・門屋光昭編『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社、平成4年）による。。 本殿における例祭の後に土俵へ移り、正面に張った幕の前に宮司や検査役が着座し、南北両成島集落の力士が両親に抱かれて北方、南方それぞれの位置に着座してから開始となる。所役は両集落から「相撲親方」各1名、「酒番」各1名、「酌立」各2名の計8名と力士12名。親方役は世襲で[[紋付]]、[[袴]]に[[裃]]を着し、酒番は紋付、袴姿、酌立は過去に角力式を経験した12歳の男子が選ばれる（裃に袴姿）。親方と酒番は襟に[[扇子]]を挿す。 初めに酌立による杯事が7度、5度、3度と3回繰り返される間に親方が「扇取組行事」「手取行事」を行い、それが終わると十二番角力の取組となる。酒番が親から幼児を抱き取って親方へ渡すと、親方は幼児を抱いて土俵中央に進んで向かい合い、「ヨォヨォ」の掛け声で3度双方の抱く幼児の顔を合わせ、先に泣き出した側を負けとする。6番の取組が終わると相手を替えて再度6番の取組を行い、都合12番の取組を終えると土俵から四方に[[餅]]が撒かれる。両集落の勝敗は12番を総合して判じ、勝った側の集落には豊作が齎されるという。 本来は作占（豊凶占い）の神事であるが、現在は幼児の成長を祈る意も込められる[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html 岩手県神道青年会「三熊野神社」]（平成23年10月2日閲覧）。。 伝えによると、坂上田村麻呂が戦勝の祝宴をこの地で開いた際に両集落の若者に相撲を取らせ、田村麻呂が勝った側の集落に豊作を約束した事に由来する神事であるといい、その後も両集落の青年によって行われて来たが、勝利した集落の豊作が約束されているために流血を見る場合もあり、[[宝永]]3年（[[1706年]]）から幼児による泣き相撲に変えられたという。[[平成]]5年（[[1993年]]）に花巻市の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。 現代になって5月上旬の3日間に毘沙門堂で行われる「毘沙門まつり」においても「全国泣き相撲大会」と称して催されるようになったが、そちらは参加資格を2歳以下の男女とし、全国からの参加者を募るものである。 == 社殿 == 本殿は桁行3間梁間2間の[[切妻造]]平入の[[身舎]]前面に桁行3間の吹放ちの庇が接続し、さらに庇中央に1間幅の[[向拝]]を設ける。以上、屋根は鉄板葺で庇の屋根は身舎屋根から一続きに葺き下ろす[[流造]]の形式は採らずに折れながら接続する形態を採る。なお、身舎の棟には[[千木・鰹木]]を備える。建築年代は不詳ながら、庇や向拝の[[虹梁]]の[[木鼻]]に見られる[[絵様]]彫刻に[[室町時代]]の地方的作風が窺える事からその時代に遡るものと推定され、当時成立したと見られる特異な社殿形態を留めている。庇と向拝柱との繋ぎとして下端に3箇所の[[茨 (建築)|茨（いばら）]]のある[[アーチ|アーチ状]]の変形[[海老虹梁]]を用いている点も珍しく、意匠的に見ても優れたものがあり、[[昭和]]54年（[[1979年]]）に岩手県の有形文化財に指定された。ただしその際の修復で屋根形状の原形を失っている点は惜しまれる日本建築学会編『総覧日本の建築』第1巻／北海道・東北（新建築社、1986年）。。 拝殿は桁行梁間とも3間の[[入母屋造]]平入。 == 文化財 == （件名後の括弧内は指定の種別と年月日） '''岩手県指定有形文化財''' * 本殿 - 昭和54年2月27日指定 '''花巻市指定無形民俗文化財''' * 十二番角力式 - 平成5年4月15日指定 == 成島毘沙門堂 == [[File:Narushima Bisyamonndo,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|thumb|成島毘沙門堂（2018年10月）]] 法人名は'''毘沙門堂'''（びしゃもんどう）。[[坂上田村麻呂]]または[[円仁]]の[[開山 (仏教)#開基|開基]]と伝えるが、創建の経緯は定かでない。 5月上旬の3日間に「毘沙門まつり」が開催され、全国から2歳以下の幼児（性別不問）の参加を募って土俵上で対面させ、先に泣いた方を負けとする「全国泣き相撲大会」が行われる。 === 成島毘沙門堂の文化財 === '''重要文化財（国指定）''' * 毘沙門堂 - 様式上、室町時代後期の建立と推定される。方三間、[[寄棟造]]、鉄板葺（もと[[茅葺]]）の仏堂。内部に安置されていた兜跋毘沙門天像は、収蔵庫に移されている。 * 木造[[兜跋毘沙門天]]立像（附:二鬼坐像 2躯） - [[平安時代]]中期の作。本像のように、地天の両掌の上に立つ形式の毘沙門天像を「兜跋（とばつ）毘沙門天」と称する。像高3.59[[メートル]]、足下の地天像を含む総高4.73メートルで、欅の[[一木造]]。仏像の中では県下最大とされている。近年、11世紀前半に鎮守府の主導で造立されたとする説が示されている佐藤高史「岩手・三熊野神社（成島毘沙門堂）木造兜跋毘沙門天立像の造像背景をめぐって」『仏教芸術』11（仏教芸術学会編、2023年）。。二鬼坐像は[[一木造]]。 * 木造伝吉祥天立像 - 平安時代前期の作。[[吉祥天]]像とされているが、服装は通常の吉祥天とは異なり菩薩形であり、両手を胸の辺まで上げ、頭上に2つの象頭を乗せる特異な像容をもつ。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『角川日本地名大辞典 3岩手県』角川書店、昭和60年 ISBN 4-04-001030-2 * 『岩手県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系第3巻）平凡社、1990年 ISBN 4-582-49003-4 * 「新指定の文化財」『月刊文化財』323号、第一法規、1990年 * 久野健編『図説仏像巡礼事典』（新訂版）、山川出版社、1994年 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html 三熊野神社]（岩手県神道青年会） * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ciss/bunkazai/detail/id/27 熊野神社本殿]・[http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html 三熊野神社の泣き相撲]（岩手県政策地域部NPO・文化国際課「いわての文化情報大事典」） {{神道 横}} {{リダイレクトの所属カテゴリ |header=この記事は以下のカテゴリでも参照できます |redirect1=成島寺 |1-1=岩手県の寺 |1-2=廃寺 |1-3=花巻市の歴史 |1-4=神仏分離 }} {{DEFAULTSORT:みくまのしんしや}} [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:岩手県の旧村社]] [[Category:岩手県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:岩手県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:田村語り|社みくまのしんしや]] [[Category:毘沙門天]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:花巻市の歴史]] [[Category:花巻市の建築物]]

revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=2015年10月27日 (火) 01:04 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 三熊野神社 |画像 = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 (2018年10月){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} |所在地 = [[岩手県]][[花巻市]]東和町北成島5区1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[伊弉冉命]]
[[事解男命]]
[[速玉男命]] |社格 = 旧[[村社]] |創建 = 伝[[延暦]]21年（[[802年]]） |本殿 = 切妻造鉄板葺 |別名 = 熊野神社 |札所等 = |例祭 = 9月19日 |神事 = 十二番角力式（泣き相撲）（9月19日）
全国泣き相撲大会（4月下旬） |地図={{Location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=三熊野神社|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}}}} '''三熊野神社'''（みくまのじんじゃ）は、[[岩手県]][[花巻市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]である。正式名称は'''熊野神社'''（くまのじんじゃ）。 == 祭神 == [[紀伊国]]の[[熊野三山]]から[[勧請]]されたと伝える[[伊弉冉命]]、[[事解男命]]、[[速玉男命]]の3柱を祀る。 == 歴史 == 社伝によれば[[坂上田村麻呂]]が対[[蝦夷]]戦争（[[蝦夷征討]]）に際して、征矢立（せいやたて）の森に登って紀伊の熊野三山に戦勝を祈願し『岩手県の地名』。、戦勝後の[[延暦]]21年（[[802年]]）にこの地に三山の神を勧請して創祀したという。また、[[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]）に[[源義家]]が[[前九年の役]]で[[安倍貞任]]を追撃した際には[[鏑矢]]を奉納して戦勝を祈願し、[[奥羽]]平定を叶えたともいう。 中世には[[和賀氏]]から[[社領]]70[[石 (単位)|石]]を寄進され、[[江戸時代]]になると[[元和 (日本)|元和]]4年（[[1618年]]）に[[南部藩]]藩主[[南部利直]]より社領23石が安堵された。 近世には「熊野権現宮」と呼ばれ、境内に隣接する毘沙門堂（成島毘沙門堂）の[[鎮守]]とされ、古くからともに'''熊野山成島寺'''（じょうとうじ）を[[別当]]としていたが、[[明治]]初年（19世紀後葉）の[[神仏分離]]で成島寺を廃寺とした際に独立し、毘沙門堂とも分離した。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。 == 祭祀 == [[File:Mikmano-jinjya dohyo,Hanamaki.jpg|thumb|泣き相撲土俵 (2018年10月)]] 9月19日の例祭に奉納される'''十二番角力式'''（じゅうにばんすもうしき）は[[幼児]]による'''泣き相撲'''として知られる角力（[[相撲]]）神事で、鎮座地の南を西流する[[猿ヶ石川]]を境に南北に分かれる北南両成島集落のそれぞれから長男で[[数え年]]2歳の幼児6名宛を[[力士]]として選び、本殿脇の[[土俵]]上で対面させる以下、本節は別注記を除いて門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」（高橋秀雄・門屋光昭編『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社、平成4年）による。。 本殿における例祭の後に土俵へ移り、正面に張った幕の前に宮司や検査役が着座し、南北両成島集落の力士が両親に抱かれて北方、南方それぞれの位置に着座してから開始となる。所役は両集落から「相撲親方」各1名、「酒番」各1名、「酌立」各2名の計8名と力士12名。親方役は世襲で[[紋付]]、[[袴]]に[[裃]]を着し、酒番は紋付、袴姿、酌立は過去に角力式を経験した12歳の男子が選ばれる（裃に袴姿）。親方と酒番は襟に[[扇子]]を挿す。 初めに酌立による杯事が7度、5度、3度と3回繰り返される間に親方が「扇取組行事」「手取行事」を行い、それが終わると十二番角力の取組となる。酒番が親から幼児を抱き取って親方へ渡すと、親方は幼児を抱いて土俵中央に進んで向かい合い、「ヨォヨォ」の掛け声で3度双方の抱く幼児の顔を合わせ、先に泣き出した側を負けとする。6番の取組が終わると相手を替えて再度6番の取組を行い、都合12番の取組を終えると土俵から四方に[[餅]]が撒かれる。両集落の勝敗は12番を総合して判じ、勝った側の集落には豊作が齎されるという。 本来は作占（豊凶占い）の神事であるが、現在は幼児の成長を祈る意も込められる[http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html 岩手県神道青年会「三熊野神社」]（平成23年10月2日閲覧）。。 伝えによると、坂上田村麻呂が戦勝の祝宴をこの地で開いた際に両集落の若者に相撲を取らせ、田村麻呂が勝った側の集落に豊作を約束した事に由来する神事であるといい、その後も両集落の青年によって行われて来たが、勝利した集落の豊作が約束されているために流血を見る場合もあり、[[宝永]]3年（[[1706年]]）から幼児による泣き相撲に変えられたという。[[平成]]5年（[[1993年]]）に花巻市の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。 現代になって5月上旬の3日間に毘沙門堂で行われる「毘沙門まつり」においても「全国泣き相撲大会」と称して催されるようになったが、そちらは参加資格を2歳以下の男女とし、全国からの参加者を募るものである。 == 社殿 == 本殿は桁行3間梁間2間の[[切妻造]]平入の[[身舎]]前面に桁行3間の吹放ちの庇が接続し、さらに庇中央に1間幅の[[向拝]]を設ける。以上、屋根は鉄板葺で庇の屋根は身舎屋根から一続きに葺き下ろす[[流造]]の形式は採らずに折れながら接続する形態を採る。なお、身舎の棟には[[千木・鰹木]]を備える。建築年代は不詳ながら、庇や向拝の[[虹梁]]の[[木鼻]]に見られる[[絵様]]彫刻に[[室町時代]]の地方的作風が窺える事からその時代に遡るものと推定され、当時成立したと見られる特異な社殿形態を留めている。庇と向拝柱との繋ぎとして下端に3箇所の[[茨 (建築)|茨（いばら）]]のある[[アーチ|アーチ状]]の変形[[海老虹梁]]を用いている点も珍しく、意匠的に見ても優れたものがあり、[[昭和]]54年（[[1979年]]）に岩手県の有形文化財に指定された。ただしその際の修復で屋根形状の原形を失っている点は惜しまれる日本建築学会編『総覧日本の建築』第1巻／北海道・東北（新建築社、1986年）。。 拝殿は桁行梁間とも3間の[[入母屋造]]平入。 == 文化財 == （件名後の括弧内は指定の種別と年月日） '''岩手県指定有形文化財''' * 本殿 - 昭和54年2月27日指定 '''花巻市指定無形民俗文化財''' * 十二番角力式 - 平成5年4月15日指定 == 成島毘沙門堂 == [[File:Narushima Bisyamonndo,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|thumb|成島毘沙門堂（2018年10月）]] 法人名は'''毘沙門堂'''（びしゃもんどう）。[[坂上田村麻呂]]または[[円仁]]の[[開山 (仏教)#開基|開基]]と伝えるが、創建の経緯は定かでない。 5月上旬の3日間に「毘沙門まつり」が開催され、全国から2歳以下の幼児（性別不問）の参加を募って土俵上で対面させ、先に泣いた方を負けとする「全国泣き相撲大会」が行われる。 === 成島毘沙門堂の文化財 === '''重要文化財（国指定）''' * 毘沙門堂 - 様式上、室町時代後期の建立と推定される。方三間、[[寄棟造]]、鉄板葺（もと[[茅葺]]）の仏堂。内部に安置されていた兜跋毘沙門天像は、収蔵庫に移されている。 * 木造[[兜跋毘沙門天]]立像（附:二鬼坐像 2躯） - [[平安時代]]中期の作。本像のように、地天の両掌の上に立つ形式の毘沙門天像を「兜跋（とばつ）毘沙門天」と称する。像高3.59[[メートル]]、足下の地天像を含む総高4.73メートルで、欅の[[一木造]]。仏像の中では県下最大とされている。近年、11世紀前半に鎮守府の主導で造立されたとする説が示されている佐藤高史「岩手・三熊野神社（成島毘沙門堂）木造兜跋毘沙門天立像の造像背景をめぐって」『仏教芸術』11（仏教芸術学会編、2023年）。。二鬼坐像は[[一木造]]。 * 木造伝吉祥天立像 - 平安時代前期の作。[[吉祥天]]像とされているが、服装は通常の吉祥天とは異なり菩薩形であり、両手を胸の辺まで上げ、頭上に2つの象頭を乗せる特異な像容をもつ。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『角川日本地名大辞典 3岩手県』角川書店、昭和60年 ISBN 4-04-001030-2 * 『岩手県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系第3巻）平凡社、1990年 ISBN 4-582-49003-4 * 「新指定の文化財」『月刊文化財』323号、第一法規、1990年 * 久野健編『図説仏像巡礼事典』（新訂版）、山川出版社、1994年 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html 三熊野神社]（岩手県神道青年会） * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ciss/bunkazai/detail/id/27 熊野神社本殿]・[http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html 三熊野神社の泣き相撲]（岩手県政策地域部NPO・文化国際課「いわての文化情報大事典」） {{神道 横}} {{リダイレクトの所属カテゴリ |header=この記事は以下のカテゴリでも参照できます |redirect1=成島寺 |1-1=岩手県の寺 |1-2=廃寺 |1-3=花巻市の歴史 |1-4=神仏分離 }} {{DEFAULTSORT:みくまのしんしや}} [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:岩手県の旧村社]] [[Category:岩手県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:岩手県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:田村語り|社みくまのしんしや]] [[Category:毘沙門天]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:花巻市の歴史]] [[Category:花巻市の建築物]]

{{Shrine | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
Honden (October 2018){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = Former village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg|270px]]
Honden (October 2018){{Maplink2|zoom=11|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=220|type=point|frame-latitude=39.36861|frame-longitude=141.15755|coord={{coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51|E}}}} | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39|21|56.3|N|141|11|51.0|E|landmark_region:JP-03|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = Former village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=draft}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|12=draft}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|12=draft}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|12=draft}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=draft}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏|12=draft}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=draft}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Hanamaki Buildings]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshiie|qq=}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Nine Years' War|qq=}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu clan|qq=}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]]

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:岩手県の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]]

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:岩手県の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:田村語り]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]]

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[en:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[Category:岩手県の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:田村語り]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:花巻市の建築物]] [[Category:花巻市の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Hanamaki History]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:岩手県の旧村社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:田村語り]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:花巻市の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Hanamaki]], [[Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Village Shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu Shugo]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:田村語り]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:花巻市の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:田村語り]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:花巻市の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:田村語り]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:花巻の Municipal History]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]
