National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11356652]] Wikidata name: Mikumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|qid=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|qid=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|qid=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Mikumano Shrine (Hanamaki)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[d:" to "[[da:d:"
revision.text:
[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11356652]] Wikidata name: Mikumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|WD=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|WD=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|qq=|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}}

revision.text:
[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11356652]] Wikidata name: Mikumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|WD=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|WD=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11356652]] Wikidata name: Mikumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|WD=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|WD=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11356652]] Wikidata name: Mikumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|WD=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|WD=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}}

The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

revision.text:
[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11356652]] Wikidata name: Mikumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|WD=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|WD=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]]. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 21 (802) by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] as part of his campaign against the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]]. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]] dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the [[:en:Former Nine Years' War|Former Nine Years' War]] (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the [[:en:Nanbu clan|Nanbu clan]] during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|WD=Q11568925}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|WD=Q317827}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Waga clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Q11418215|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Mikumano Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Mikumano Shrine,Hanamaki,Iwate.jpg | location = Higashiwamachi, Narisawa 5-ku-1, [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|39.36563889|N|141.1975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Izanami|qid=Q682306|1=Izanami}}; {{ill|Kumano Gongen|qid=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} | Shrine Rank = Former Village shrine | Foundation = Tradition {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|qid=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 21 (802) | Main hall = Kirizuma-zukuri with iron sheet roofing | alternate_name = Kumano Shrine | Annual festival = September 19 | Rituals = Juuniban Sumo Ceremony (Crying Sumo, September 19)
National Crying Sumo Tournament (late April) | Map = {{location map|Japan Iwate|lat_deg=39|lat_min=21|lat_sec=56.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=11|lon_sec=51.0|label=Mikumano Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} | location = {{ill|Hanamaki|qid=Q317827|1=Hanamaki, Iwate}} }} '''Mikumano Shrine''' (三熊野神社, Mikumano Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Hanamaki|Hanamaki]], {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}. The official name is '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社). == Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja|事解男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, and {{ill|Kumano Gongen|lt=Kumano Gongen|lt=Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速玉男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, believed to have been transferred from the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}. == History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|qid=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 21 (802) by {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|qid=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} as part of his campaign against the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|qid=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}}. It is said that he prayed for victory at a grove called Seiyatate Mori, invoking the Kumano deities from Kii Province.『岩手県の地名』。 In later years, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|qid=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} dedicated a ceremonial arrow to pray for victory during the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|qid=Q1187448|lt=Former Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}} (1051–1063) against Abe Sadato. The shrine received lands from the {{ill|Waga clan|ja|和賀氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waga clan|1=Q11418215|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11418215}} during the medieval period and later from the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|qid=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during the Edo period. After the Meiji Restoration, the shrine became independent from its former affiliation with the {{ill|Jōto-ji Temple|ja|熊野山成島寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōto-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} due to the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy. It was designated a village shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == Rituals == The shrine is best known for its unique ritual, the '''Juuniban Sumo Ceremony''' (Twelve-Round Sumo Ceremony), commonly referred to as "Crying Sumo." It involves toddlers from two local communities—North Narisawa and South Narisawa—competing on a dohyo (sumo ring) to see which child cries first.門屋光昭「熊野神社の泣き相撲」『祭礼行事・岩手県』桜楓社。 According to local lore, this ritual was initiated by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, who celebrated his military victories by holding sumo matches. Over time, the event transitioned into a symbolic ritual involving children. == Architecture == The main hall (honden) is an unusual structure with features suggesting {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} origins. It has a kirizuma-zukuri style with iron roofing and includes elements such as transformed ebikoryo (decorative beams) with unique carvings.『総覧日本の建築』日本建築学会。 == Cultural Properties == * '''Iwate Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties''' ** Main Hall - Designated on February 27, 1979. * '''Hanamaki City Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties''' ** Juuniban Sumo Ceremony - Designated on April 15, 1993. == References == * 『岩手県の地名』 (Japanese Place name Dictionary, Iwate Volume). Heibonsha, 1990. == External Links == * [http://www.ganshinsei.jp/hanamakishi/272kumanojinja.html Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Shrine Association)] * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/dentou/maturi/maturi/maturi_021.html Crying Sumo at Mikumano Shrine (Iwate Cultural Assets Database)] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|三熊野神社 (花巻市)|version=100502381|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:三熊野神社 (花巻市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11356652}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hanamaki, Iwate]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Iwate Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hanamaki]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Tamura storytelling]] [[Category:Vaiśravaṇa]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
